Vance (Cyrus R. and Grace Sloane) Papers

did.unittitle: Cyrus R. and Grace Sloane Vance papers
did.abstract: The Vance papers primarily document Cyrus R. Vance's professional and personal activities. Of particular significance are background materials, correspondence, position papers, and handwritten meeting notes relating to SALT II negotiation between the United States and the Soviet Union; the Camp David Summit and the signing of the Middle East Peace Treaty; diplomatic relations with the Far East, especially China; and negotiations to release the American hostages in Iran. Proposals, reports, handwritten notes, and correspondence provide insight into the dispute between Greece and Turkey over Cyprus in 1967, federal recovery assistance to Detroit after the riot of 1967, and the Paris Peace Talks on Vietnam in 1968. Governmental statements and commentaries, draft bills, and Senate committee background materials from 1958 document Vance's involvement in the creation of the National Aeronautics and Space Agency (NASA). Extensive files of position papers, project proposals, meeting minutes, reports, publications, and handwritten notes document Vance's involvement with various events and prestigious organizations, following his resignation from the Carter administration. The collection also contains manuscript drafts used for Vance's book Hard Choices: Critical Years in America's Foreign Policy. Grace Sloane Vance's papers document her trip with Rosalynn Carter to Latin America in 1977. Her work throughout the 1960s with Widening Horizons can be traced through correspondence, working papers, minutes, and notes.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Gift of Cyrus R. and Grace Sloane Vance, 1993, 1995-1998, 2002. Gift of Camilla Vance, Elsie Nicoll Vance, Grace Roberts Vance, and Cyrus Roberts Vance, Jr., 2008.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: Classified documents from Series I-V (boxes 83-87)are restricted pending review by the National Archives and Records Administration. Yale University files located in boxes 65-74 of Accession 1996-M-152 are restricted until January 1, 2039 due to University policy set by the Yale Corporation. Correspondence located in box 64 of Accession 1996-M-152 is closed to researchers as it is undergoing declassification review by the federal government. Boxes 19 and 20 of Accession 1998-M-122 are closed until January 1, 2019 per agreement with the donor. Box 21 of Accession 1998-M-122 is closed until January 1, 2046 as established by Yale Corporation regulations.. Boxes 8, 9, and 29 of Accession 2008-M-080 are closed until January 1, 2019. Boxes 10-11 of Accession 2008-M-080 are closed until January 1, 2036. Series VII, Scrapbooks, 1961-1980 and Series IX, Objects and Memorabilia, 1964-1988 may be viewed only under the supervision of a senior staff member. Original audiovisual materials, as well as preservation and duplicating masters, may not be played. Researchers must consult use copies, or if none exist must pay for a use copy, which is retained by the repository. Researchers wishing to obtain an additional copy for their personal use should consult Copying Services information on the Manuscripts and Archives web site. Copies of commercially produced audiovisual materials contained in this collection cannot be made for researcher use outside of the repository.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: Copyright has been transferred to Yale University for unpublished materials authored or otherwise produced by the creator(s) of this collection. Copyright status for other collection materials is unknown. Transmission or reproduction of materials protected by U.S. Copyright Law (Title 17, U.S.C.) beyond that allowed by fair use requires the written permission of the copyright owners. Works not in the public domain cannot be commercially exploited without permission of the copyright owners. Responsibility for any use rests exclusively with the user.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Cyrus R. and Grace Sloane Vance Papers (MS 1664). Manuscripts and Archives, Yale University Library.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Arranged in ten series and six additions: I. Government Service Papers (Kennedy and Johnson Presidential Administrations), 1960-1977. II. Government Service Papers (Carter Presidential Administration), 1976-1981. III. Papers on Professional and Personal Activities, 1957-1992. IV. Grace Sloane Vance Papers, 1957-1989. V. Personal and Routine Correspondence, 1960-1990. VI. Photographs, 1958-1988. VII. Scrapbooks, 1961-1980. VIII. Clippings, 1958-1990. IX. Objects and Memorabilia, 1964-1980. X. Audio and Video Tapes, 1963-1980.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The Cyrus R. Vance and Grace Sloane Vance Papers primarily document Cyrus R. Vance's professional and personal activities beginning in 1957. Of particular significance are the materials related to his governmental service. Background materials, correspondence, position papers, and handwritten meeting notes from his tenure as secretary of state document SALT II negotiations between the United States and the Soviet Union, the Camp David Summit with Israel and Egypt and the signing of the Middle East Peace Treaty, diplomatic relations with the Far East, especially China, and negotiations to release the American hostages in Iran. Proposals, reports, handwritten notes, and correspondence provide insight into Vance's important work in the Johnson administration as the president's special representative to settle the dispute between Greece and Turkey over Cyprus in 1967, to provide federal recovery assistance to Detroit after the riot in the Summer of 1967, and to negotiate for a cease-fire at the Peace Talks on Vietnam in 1968.

Governmental statements and commentaries, draft bills, and Senate committee background materials from 1958 document Vance's involvement in the creation of the National Aeronautics and Space Agency (NASA). Extensive files of position papers, project proposals, meeting minutes, reports, publications, and handwritten notes document Vance's involvement in various organizations, including the Aspen Institute, the Center for National Policy, the Council on Foreign Relations, the Palme Commission on Disarmament and Security Issues, and the Williamsburg Conferences. Vance's papers also contain manuscript drafts and research notes used for Hard Choices: Critical Years in America's Foreign Policy.

Grace Sloane Vance's papers document her trip with First Lady Rosalynn Carter to Latin America in 1977 through briefings files and her own notes. Her work throughout the 1960s with Widening Horizons, a Washington D.C. public schools Urban Service Corps project designed to introduce children to professional life, culture, and career opportunities, can be traced through correspondence, working papers, minutes, and notes.

Photographs, scrapbooks, and audio-tapes document many domestic and international events related to Vance's governmental service. Newspaper clippings kept throughout Vance's political career supply general information about key events and initiatives which characterize the Kennedy, Johnson, and Carter presidential administrations. Vance's correspondence files contain exchanges with prominent governmental figures and foreign heads of state, and his collection of diplomatic gifts, mementos, citations, and commemorative plaques reflect his legacy of diplomatic service.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Cyrus Roberts Vance was born 27 March 1917, in Clarksburg, West Virginia, to John Carl and Amy (Roberts) Vance. A year later, the family moved to Bronxville, New York, where they resided until 1922 when Vance's father unexpectedly died of pneumonia. Both Vance brothers, Cyrus and John, spent the following year in Switzerland attending private school at the Institut Sillig. Vance also spent time with his uncle John W. Davis, who was President Wilson's ambassador to the Court of St. James, and then the Democrat candidate for president in 1924.

From 1930 to 1935, Vance prepared at the Kent School, Kent, Connecticut. He then attended Yale University, graduating in 1939 with a B.A. in Economics. While an undergraduate, he was a member of Scroll and Key, the Torch Honor Society, the hockey team, and the Fence Club. He continued his studies at the Yale Law School and earned an LL.B. with honors in 1942. Later that year, he joined the military, serving as a World War II naval gunnery officer in the Atlantic and Pacific Oceans. He left the Navy in 1946 with the rank of lieutenant senior grade .

In 1947, Vance worked as an assistant to the president of the Mead Corporation and passed the New York State Bar. He then joined the law firm of Simpson, Thacher & Bartlett, and specialized in civil litigation. In addition to legal work, he began governmental service in 1957 as associate counsel to the Senate Armed Forces Preparedness Investigation Subcommittee, serving alongside Texas Senator Lyndon Baines Johnson. In 1958, Vance was appointed consulting counsel to the Senate Committee on Space and Aeronautics and helped to draft the National Space Act of 1958, which led to the creation of the National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA).

In 1961, President John F. Kennedy appointed Vance general counsel for the Defense Department. He provided important assistance to Kennedy in shaping Cuban policy after the Bay of Pigs incident. The President promoted him to secretary of the army the following year. Journalists reported that Kennedy had also intended to promote Vance to deputy secretary of defense, but President Johnson was actually the one to issue the promotion in January 1964, after President Kennedy was assassinated. Reporting to Secretary of Defense Robert S. McNamara, Vance negotiated and calmed political crises in the Dominican Republic and in the Panama Canal zone. In 1967, Vance resigned his post.

President Johnson continued to engage Vance's diplomatic expertise, even after his resignation. Vance secured federal recovery assistance for Detroit after the July 1967 riot. In November, he negotiated between Greece and Turkey to avert war in Cyprus. In 1968, Johnson made him an emissary to Korea and then to Washington D.C. during the riot following the assassination of Martin Luther King, Jr. In April, the President appointed him co-negotiator with Ambassador W. Averell Harriman for the Paris Peace Talks on Vietnam, at which Vance attempted to negotiate a cease-fire. He resigned from this position in 1969, after President Richard Nixon took office.

Vance returned to Simpson, Thacher & Bartlett in 1969 and worked extensively in the private and public sectors. From 1970 to 1972, he was a member of the Commission to Investigate Alleged Police Corruption in New York City. In the early 1970s, he served as chairman of the UNA (United Nations Association) - USA Policy Studies Committee, and from 1974 to 1976 he served as New York State Bar President.

In December 1976, President-elect James E. (Jimmy) Carter designated Vance the next secretary of state. Once in office, Vance worked to improve United States - Soviet relations, which led to the signing of the SALT II agreement in 1979. He also negotiated and coordinated Middle East peace efforts alongside Carter, which led to the Camp David Summit in 1978 and the Israeli-Egyptian peace treaty signing in 1979. When Americans were taken hostage in Iran on 4 November 1979, Vance focused on negotiating their release. On 28 April 1980, Vance unexpectedly resigned. Mainly, he was opposed to the ill-fated hostage rescue attempt in Iran and he sensed that the extensive use of force would make the United States dangerously vulnerable to further acts of hostility. Chronicling his work in the Carter administration, Vance, in 1983, wrote Hard Choices: Critical Years in America's Foreign Policy , which he explained as "...a story of our country and those who led it during four critical and turbulent years [1977-1980]."

Vance resumed working in the private and public sectors after his resignation. He became active in the Council on Foreign Relations, the Center for National Policy, the Palme Commission on Disarmament and Security Issues, and the Williamsburg Conferences, which address economic issues in the Asia-Pacific region. He and Lord David Owen served in 1993 as United Nations special envoys to the former Yugoslavia to advise on peace-making efforts.

In recognition of his achievements and distinguished national service, Vance has received many awards. Yale University gave him an honorary degree in 1968 and elected him to the Yale Corporation. In 1969, he was awarded the Medal of Freedom. During the 1970s and 1980s, he received honorary degrees from Salem College, Brandeis University, West Virginia Wesleyan College, Amherst College, General Theological Seminary, Colgate University, Harvard University, Williams College, University of Notre Dame, Mount Holyoke College, University of Haifa, Davidson College, and Brown University. In 1994, Vance was knighted to the British court.

Grace Sloane Vance was born 2 June 1918 in New York City. Her father was J. W. Sloane of the W. And J. Sloane Company, a furniture business. She attended Bryn Mawr College and the Parsons School of Design before marrying Cyrus Vance on 15 February 1947. During the 1960s, she was vice-chairman of the Tom Sawyer Project, which, in association with the Kennedy Center for the Performing Arts, selected children from around the country to create and to paint large panels representing their home states. She was also director of Widening Horizons, a project of the D.C. public schools Urban Service Corps, which allowed teenagers to experience governmental and business professions, cultural events, and educational opportunities first hand through job fairs, summer camps, and field trips. During the Carter presidency, she attended foreign and domestic diplomatic events, and, in 1977, traveled to Latin America with First Lady Rosalynn Carter to relate the administration's policies and to foster good will. During the 1980s, she was active in the work of the Foreign Policy Association. Vance died on March 22, 2008.
The Vances have five children: Elsie Nicoll, Amy Sloane, Grace Roberts, Camilla, and Cyrus Roberts.

Attended Kent School, Kent, Connecticut
Attended Yale College, B.A., Economics major. Member of Scroll and Key, the hockey team, Torch Honor Society, and the Fence Club
Attended Yale Law School, LL.B. with honors
United States Navy: Gunnery Officer-Destroyer service aboard the U.S.S. Hale, achieved lieutenant senior grade
Served as assistant to the president of the Mead Corporation
Admitted to the New York State Bar Association. Joined the law firm of Simpson, Thacher & Bartlett, in New York, and specialized in civil litigation
Married Grace Elsie Sloane
Served as associate counsel to the Senate Armed Forces Preparedness Investigation Subcommittee and began acquaintanceship with Lyndon Baines Johnson

Served as consulting counsel to the Senate Committee on Space and Aeronautics. Helped to draft the National Space Act of 1958, which led to the creation of the National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA)

Sworn in as general counsel to the Defense Department in the Kennedy Administration under Secretary Robert S. McNamara, active in formulating United States policy toward Cuba following the Bay of Pigs invasion
Promoted to secretary of the army
Appointed as deputy secretary of defense in the Johnson administration
Served as special representative to help calm the crisis in the Dominican Republic
Served as Defense Department representative on the Control Committee dealing with the Middle East crisis
Resigned as deputy secretary of defense
Served as President Johnson's emissary to the Detroit riot
Appointed as special representative to mediate between Greece and Turkey over Cyprus
Appointed as President Johnson's emissary to Korea
Served on President Johnson's behalf to help calm the Washington D.C. riot, which followed the assassination of Martin Luther King, Jr.
Elected fellow of the Yale Corporation, Yale University
Served as United States negotiator with W. Averell Harriman at the Paris Peace Talks on Vietnam
Awarded the Medal of Freedom
Declined post of assistant secretary of state to the Nixon administration under Secretary of State William P. Rogers
Served on the Commission to Investigate Alleged Police Corruption in New York City
Served as president of the Association of the Bar of the City of New York
Designated as secretary of state by President-Elect Carter
Sworn in as the 59th Secretary of State
Peace agreement between Egypt and Israel signed following the Camp David accords
SALT II agreement signed
Americans taken hostage in Iran
Resigned as secretary of state
Participated in the Palme Commission on Disarmament and Security Issues
Participated in the Aspen Institute for Humanistic Studies
Williamsburg Conferences participation
Co-authored the Vance-Owen letter to NATO foreign ministers on creating nuclear-free zones at the East-West frontier
Hard Choices published
Delivered policy statement to the Senate Foreign Relations Committee at the Iran/Contra Arms Deal Hearings
Appointed as United Nations special envoy with Lord David Owen to the former Yugoslavia
Cyrus Vance died in New York.

did.unittitle: Government Service Papers (Kennedy and Johnson Presidential Administrations)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series is arranged alphabetically by subject and contains official documents and correspondence related to Cyrus R. Vance's work as Defense Department general counsel, 1961-1962, as secretary of the army, 1962-1964, and as deputy secretary of defense, 1964-1967. In addition, a number of files document Vance's special appointments under President Johnson, 1967-1969, after Vance resigned from the Defense Department. Files on the Cyprus Crisis of 1967, and on related follow-up discussions in the early 1970s, consist of draft peace proposals, negotiation notes, correspondence, published news articles and bulletins, and letters supporting Vance's diplomatic accomplishments. These materials detail Vance's diplomatic ideas and priorities, reveal major figures involved in the negotiations, and document steps toward writing the final Greek-Turkish peace proposal. The Detroit riot of 1967 is documented in event briefing transcripts, summaries, chronologies, talking points for the Commission on Civil Disorders, military strategy sheets, and lists of victims and volunteers. Also contained in these files are riot summary reports written by Vance, as well as the Detroit emergency recovery program proposal. Materials related to the Paris Peace Talks on Vietnam, 1968-1969, mostly contain Vance's handwritten meeting and personal notes, which reveal his negotiation tactics and approach to working through frequent deadlock. The correspondence files (all incoming/outgoing unless otherwise noted) contain congratulatory messages on Vance's governmental appointments, as well as President Kennedy's letter appointing Vance as secretary of the army, and President Johnson's letter explaining Vance's ambassadorial role in Paris at the peace talks. The remaining files contain information on trips, conferences, ceremonies, and daily routines.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series is arranged alphabetically by subject and chronicles foreign policy priorities, initiatives, and diplomatic progress in the form of comprehensive notes and official documents which focus on Soviet, Middle East, and Far East relations. The Camp David accords with Egypt and Israel and the subsequent peace treaty signing are documented in a file containing a draft of the peace treaty with Anwar Sadat's annotations, commemorative booklets presenting the final agreement and summarizing the negotiations, correspondence, notes, and event schedules. Vance's extensive collection of handwritten notes summarize governmental and diplomatic meetings and outline foreign policy goals and objectives, especially pertaining to the Middle East, Asia, and the former Soviet Union. These notes also supplement existing published materials regarding the Carter administration's diplomatic agenda. Background articles, position and briefing papers, speeches, and correspondence from the Democrat party perspective document the 1976 Carter presidential campaign. The presidential transition papers contain background materials, information on proposed and chosen Carter administration employees, writings on campaign promises, Vance's meeting notes as secretary of state designate, and a report prepared by Vance on a proposed foreign policy conference with President-elect Carter's annotations and responses. Supportive remarks written by prominent national and international figures, State Department employees, and acquaintances to Vance when he resigned as secretary of state are documented in the resignation correspondence files, 1980. The remaining folders generally contain trip materials, daily calendars, and office papers. All correspondence are incoming/outgoing, unless otherwise noted.

did.unittitle: Papers on Professional and Personal Activities

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Vance's activities outside of his official work under Presidents Kennedy, Johnson, and Carter are documented in this series, alphabetically arranged by subject. Draft bills, statements, commentaries, Senate committee background materials, and memoranda on the 1958 initiative to create the National Aeronautics and Space Agency (NASA) are contained in the Armed Forces Senate Committee materials and in the National Aeronautics and Space Act records. The activities of the Aspen Institute, an organization for the discussion of problems related to various civilizations, regions, countries, religions, and economies are documented in articles, presentation materials, correspondence, fund-raising publications, meeting summaries, reports, and project proposals, 1981-1984. Vance's role in the Center for National Policy, 1980-1985, is outlined in files of development reports, meeting minutes, correspondence, project proposals, and background publications. The consideration of economic initiatives and new business relations with Southeast Asia, along with the investigation of relevant legal issues, are documented in the China and Shanghai Conferences, 1984-1985, for which Vance represented his law firm Simpson, Thacher & Bartlett. Reports, memoranda, meeting agendas and summaries document Vance's leadership in the Council on Foreign Relations, 1969-1987; within this set are four folders, 1975-1976, on the council's 1980s Project, which focused on nuclear weapons issues.

Series III also contains extensive Iran Contra background materials in the form of published reports, articles, time lines, and handwritten notes used by Vance for his 1987 testimony before the Senate Foreign Relations Committee. Background materials, summaries, and draft recommendations concerning the Iran hostage ordeal are contained in the Iran hostages - Presidential Commission on Hostage Compensation files, most of which were generated in 1981. Vance's work as a commissioner for the Palme Commission on Disarmament and Security Issues, known as the Independent Commission on Disarmament and Security Issues before Chairman Olaf Palme's assassination, is documented through meeting agendas and outlines, conference papers, and notes, 1980-1989. Also in this set is the 1982 Vance-Owen letter to NATO foreign ministers with various responses Vance received from prominent international figures. Schedules, press releases, background papers and reports, participant lists, correspondence, invitations and financial development plans, 1981-1986, document Vance's role as co-chairman of the Williamsburg Conferences, a series of annual meetings to discuss matters related to United States and Asian affairs. Vance's involvement in the United Nations Association, a non-profit organization dedicated to broadening public knowledge about the United Nations is documented in discussion outlines, proposals, articles, reports, testimony transcripts, and correspondence, all of which especially focus on his work as Policy Studies Committee chairman in the early and mid 1970s.

Additionally, Series III contains Vance's general and correspondence files on the Vietnam Conflict, 1968-1972, compiled after his involvement in the Paris Peace Talks, drafts for Hard Choices: Critical Years in America's Foreign Policy, 1977-1983, selected speeches and articles, and materials on other areas of Vance's professional and personal interests. All correspondence are incoming/outgoing, unless otherwise noted.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Grace Sloane Vance's papers are arranged alphabetically by subject and contain working files, meeting agendas and minutes, itineraries, critiques, brochures, and notes documenting her work as a vice-chair and chair of Widening Horizons, 1963-1970, a D.C. Urban Service Corps project which allowed teenagers to experience government and business professions, cultural events, and educational opportunities first hand through job fairs, summer camps, and field trips. This series also contains files used during her 1977 trip with Rosalynn Carter to Latin America, where they toured and delivered Carter administration diplomatic messages and policy outlines. The remaining folders contain correspondence and materials on foreign service activities, State Department events, and White House ceremonies.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series contains Vance's personal and routine response letters. The overall arrangement is chronological. Within the chronology, Vance's personal correspondence are arranged alphabetically by year or series of years. In addition to cover letters, handwritten notes, cards, and telegrams, these files contain many brochures, articles, reports, and other supplementary materials sent to Vance by governmental figures and acquaintances. Correspondence received while Vance was secretary of state, 1977-1980, feature letters from national and world leaders. Routine correspondence, 1961-1968, are arranged chronologically and contain short responses by Vance to information requests, general comments, and congratulatory messages. Files of invitations, mostly declined, 1981-1985, indicate world events i.e. conferences, institutes, meetings, ceremonies, and other special programs taking place at the time. All letters are incoming/outgoing, unless otherwise noted.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series contains color and black and white images of the Vances engaged in professional activities and ceremonial events. Each sub-series of photographs corresponds to one of the first four series in this collection. Sub-series A, Government Service in the Kennedy and Johnson Presidential Administrations contains photographs of Vance with Presidents Kennedy and Johnson, of Vance's 1966 military tour of Vietnam as secretary of the army, and various pictures taken with co-negotiator W. Averell Harriman at the Paris Peace Talks on Vietnam, 1968. The photographs found in sub-series B, Governmental Service in the Carter Presidential Administration, portray prominent national and international figures, including President James E. (Jimmy) Carter, Vice-President Walter F. Mondale, Deputy Secretary of State Warren Christopher, Pope John Paul II, Egyptian President Anwar Sadat, and Soviet Foreign Minister Andrei Gromyko. Files of oversize photographs from Vance's years of presidential service, 1962-1980, include pictures of Vance with President Lyndon B. Johnson, Vice-President Hubert H. Humphrey, President Carter, and Soviet President Leonid Brezhnev. The oversize files also contain pictures from Grace Sloane Vance's 1977 trip to Latin America with Rosalynn Carter. There are no negatives or slides in the Vance papers.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The scrapbook series mostly consists of oversize materials and is arranged chronologically within four sub-series: Government Service Papers, Commemorative Albums, Grace Sloane Vance Albums, and Miscellaneous. The large binders which comprise the first sub-series contain subject files, correspondence, photographs, clippings, press releases, programs, invitations, and schedules, 1961-1979, documenting Vance's governmental service under Presidents Kennedy, Johnson, and Carter, as well as his personal activities, 1970-1976. These notebooks supplement the materials found in Series I - IV. Scrapbooks in the remaining three sub-series commemorate official ceremonies or events, such as the 1979 Tokyo Summit and the Ninth General Assembly of the Organization of the American States, also in 1979, and show appreciation for volunteer work done by Grace Sloane Vance. Due to their fragile condition, all materials in this series may be viewed only in the presence of a Manuscripts and Archives senior staff member.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Magazine and newspaper articles comprise this series. Arranged alphabetically, these files contain clippings on Cyrus R. Vance's governmental and civic involvements, including articles on Vietnam, Middle East peace, the Carter presidential administration's foreign policy agenda, SALT II, and Hard Choices . Also, assorted articles and publicity writings document Grace Sloane Vance's work as vice-chair and chair of Widening Horizons, 1962-1966.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series contains diplomatic gifts, plaques, citations, and certificates given to the Vances in recognition of their governmental and civic service. All items within the Objects sub-series are listed alphabetically, but are packed in different orders within the boxes, according to their shapes and sizes. Many of these objects were given to Vance by Middle East heads of state during the Carter presidential administration. The remaining items, mostly plaques, citations, and certificates, are arranged alphabetically by material type. Researchers must apply for special permission to view all items within this series.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Recorded interviews, speeches, and event broadcasts related to Vance's diplomatic work and foreign service expertise are contained in this series. These items are arranged alphabetically in three sub-series: reel to reel audio tapes, videotape cassettes, and audio cassettes.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: Original audiovisual materials, as well as preservation and duplicating masters, may not be played. Researchers must consult use copies, or if none exist must pay for a use copy, which is retained by the repository. Researchers wishing to obtain an additional copy of non-commercially produced items for their personal use should consult the ordering reproductions information on the Manuscripts and Archives web site.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The small addition to Vance's government service papers most notably contain a file concerning his 1968 mission to consult with South Korean leaders about tensions with North Korea over infiltration, following the Pueblo incident and the Blue House raid. The notes file includes several pages regarding foreign policy, especially policy on covert action under the National Security Act and Hughes-Ryan Act.

The papers on professional and personal activities in this addition supplement Series III, containing files that tend to date from the 1980s and early 1990s. They primarily document Vance's activities outside of his government positions. However, these files also include Vance's daily appointment books from his years as secretary of state recording both his official and personal schedule. They consist mainly of files concerning Vance's involvement with a variety of organizations, many of which focused on legal, international, and educational issues. The files relating to the Council on Foreign Relations include detailed reports and meeting notes regarding Vance's 1987 trip to Moscow with Henry Kissinger to meet with Mikhail Gorbachev and the Soviet leadership. The Japan Society files contain correspondence and speeches about Vance's work as society chairman from 1985 to 1993.

The Bhopal disaster and Museum of Modern Art and Guernica files document some of Vance's international legal work. The Bhopal materials consist largely of research materials, but they also contain memoranda and correspondence providing legal advice to the Indian government in pursuing its liability claims for the deadly industrial accident at the Union Carbide factory in Bhopal. Memoranda and correspondence also document Vance's legal advisory role in the repatriation of Pablo Picasso's masterpiece back to Spain from the Museum of Modern Art in New York City.

The papers on professional and personal activities also include materials documenting Vance's international travels, meetings, and conference participation. There are speeches and papers presented at the 1984 Dalian conference on foreign investment in China as well as memoranda of conversation with Vietnamese leaders about conditions in Vietnam and the prospects for normalization of relations with the United States from his 1986 trip to the Far East.

Although much of the personal and routine correspondence was exchanged with professional colleagues and is related to Vance's time in government, it consists largely of ordinary requests, offers of information, and unexceptional professional communication, often involving references and departures. There are a few letters that provide more insight into his work as secretary of state. For example, a 1977 letter from Michael Blumenthal discusses Henry Kissinger's complaint about the removal of Secret Service protection. A 1979 letter from John K. Galbraith discusses policy toward Iran. More can be found in the files from the years after he left office, especially the files for 1981 which include correspondence, usually only a single letter, with Zbigniew Brzezinski, William Bundy, Ellsworth Bunker, Jimmy Carter, Sargent Shriver, and Strobe Talbot and notes from interviews with the press and researchers concerning his policymaking as secretary of state. The unidentified file for 1989 contains detailed notes concerning the Soviet policy of Glasnost from Vance's Moscow trip with Les Aspin and members of the Dartmouth Conference.
Other materials supplement Series VI (photographs), and document his service to Yale University.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The papers on professional and personal activities in this addition supplement Series III, containing files that tend to date from the late 1980s and 1990s. They consist mainly of files concerning Vance's involvement with a variety of organizations, many of which focused on international and political issues. There are a number of trip files, most of which contain little more than background material and itineraries, but the file for his 1986 trip to Vietnam includes notes of his discussions and memoranda of conversation with the Vietnamese leaders Nguyen Co Thach, Pham Van Dong, and Le Duc Tho. A copy of Vance's oral history interview with the Kennedy Library provides a detailed description of his involvement as secretary of the army with the deployment of the armed forces to enforce racial integration of the University of Mississippi in 1962 when James Meredith was admitted.

The statements and speeches document Vance's public role outside of government starting in the late 1960s and early 1970s and then again after he left the Carter administration in 1980. Almost all of the articles and published statements appeared after he left government in 1980. They illustrate Vance's continuing participation in foreign policy discussions.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This accession comprises research for Vance's book Hard Choices: Critical Years in America's Foreign Policy (1983). The bulk of the materials are note cards that list sources and quotes from the sources, as well as some annotations. There are also some request slips from the Department of State Library. While this is part of Vance's papers, these note cards were created by Vance's researcher, Eric Newsom. The note cards are in boxes with labelled tabbed cards separating each chapter/section/subsection. The note cards are arranged as created by Newsom and generally echo the structure of the book. Some folder titles occasionally use final chapter titles, while others list the topic covered. Creation dates for boxes 1-8 are from creation of the sources listed and quoted on the note cards. Box 9 materials are dated by the years Newsom performed the research.

separatedmaterial.head: Separated Materials
separatedmaterial.p: Accession 2015-M-027 originally contained some materials that are classified. They were removed and replaced with yellow "out" cards. These materials are currently in custody of the Information Security Oversight Office, National Archives and Records Administration and will be replaced in the collection once the materials are declassified.
